metadata.title: Identification of Mirror Repeats within the SWI 6 Gene of Saccharomyces cerevisiae
metadata.description: Bhardwaj, Vikash, Abstract, Namrata (2025): Identification of Mirror Repeats within the SWI 6 Gene of Saccharomyces cerevisiae. FishTaxa 37 (9): 157-163

8. Mittal, P., & Roberts, C. W. M. (2020). The SWI/SNF complex in cancer - biology, biomarkers and therapy. Nature Reviews. Clinical Oncology, 17(7), 435-448. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41571-020-0357-3

9. Pearson, W. R. (1994). Using the FASTA search algorithm to compare protein and nucleotide sequences. In Methods in Enzymology (Vol. 183, pp. 63-98). Academic Press. https://doi.org/10.1016/0076-6879(90)83005-L

10. Sidorova, J. M., Mikesell, G. E., & Breeden, L. L. (1995). Cell cycle-regulated phosphorylation of Swi6 controls its nuclear localization. Molecular Biology of the Cell, 6(12), 1641-1658. https://doi.org/10.1091/mbc.6.12.1641

13. Wright, F. (1999). The mirror-repeat method of identifying regulatory sequences in the genome. Journal of Molecular Biology, 291(1), 31-39. https://doi.org/10.1006/jmbi.1999.2831

14. Yadav, P., Kumari, J., Yadav, P., Yadav, R., Yadav, S., Sharma, D., Singh, A., Sehrawat, B., Yadav, M., & Yadav, S. (2024). Identification of mirror repeats in viral genomes using FPCB analysis: Life sciences-bioinformatics for health care. International Journal of Life Science and Pharma Research, 14(4), L12-L19. https://doi.org/10.22376/ijlpr.2024.14.4.L12-L19

metadata.title: SCIENCE BFSP CoS | WP2 | D2.4: Connecting and sharing pan-genome information across different plant platforms
metadata.description: This deliverable (D2.4) reports on the prototyping of data formats, development of API calls, and the subsequent integration and visualization of pan-genome data across established plant platforms. Led by ELIXIR UK with assistance from ELIXIR Belgium, this work was conducted under Work Package 3 (WP3) of the E-PAN project within the BFSP Commissioned Service, specifically linking the PLAZA/FRINGE comparative genomics environment with the KnetMiner knowledge graph.

metadata.title: SCIENCE BFSP CoS | WP2 | D 2.3: Prototyping and evaluation of data visualisations to represent pan-genome dynamics
metadata.description: This deliverable (D2.3) reports on the prototyping and evaluation of data visualizations for representing pan-genome dynamics, as specified in Activity 2 (T2.2) of WP2 (E-PAN) under the BFSP Commissioned Service. Building on T2.1's tool for presence/absence variation (PAV) detection, the prototypes enable exploration of structural and proteomic variation in plant pan-genomes, through web-based visualization tools. The advancements come from two parallel developments: the first within the German node (IPK and FZJ), the second within the Belgian node (VIB).

metadata.title: Assessing the Reliability of LLM Annotations in the Context of Demographic Bias and Model Explanation
metadata.description: Code and analysis comparing human annotators of varied demographics against demographically persona-prompted LLMs, using SHAP to attribute label decisions to specific text spans. Archived snapshot for the v1.0-thesis release accompanying the doctoral thesis Let Me Explain! Explainable NLP for Understanding Large Language Models (Utrecht University, 2026).

data.title: Assessing the Reliability of LLM Annotations in the Context of Demographic Bias and Model Explanation

metadata.title: [OME2026] NL-Bioimaging: National Perspective on the Use of OME in the Data Life Cycle
metadata.description:
Abstract: Netherlands Bioimaging (NL-BI) is the Dutch national research infrastructure for advanced light microscopy, supporting the bioimaging community in close collaboration with the Dutch Society for Microscopy (NVvM). One of the central aims of NL-BI is to develop common approaches, tools and resources for making Netherlands bioimaging data and analyses FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable). 

By building on existing international efforts, tools and standards in bioimaging, we provide a distributed national infrastructure, with data management at each local node, and metadata sharing at the national level. We use the REMBI (Recommended Metadata for Biological Images) and ISA (Investigations, Studies and Assay) metadata standards for capturing and managing experimental details, and BIOMERO (an extension of OMERO) for storing and sharing biomages. BIOMERO provides easy integration with high-performance compute (HPC) clusters and allows researcher to run reproducible analysis workflows on HPC through a user-friendly interface. An automated data importer, and new features for metadata capture, annotation and utilisation helps us support the FAIR data life cycle for bioimaging experiments.

meeting.title: 19th OME Community Meeting
meeting.place: Haus der Universität, Heinrich-Heine-Universität, Düsseldorf, Germany
meeting.session: Session4: OME as Part of Research Infrastructures

metadata.title: Annotation of inverted repeats displaying features of pble STIR or IR in the hg38, mm10, galGal5 and dr11 genome models
metadata.description: Annotation of inverted repeats displaying features of pble STIR or IR in the hg38 genome model. The annotation of pble-like inner inverted repeats was done using Palindrome (EMBOSS package). The output file was then filtered using pal2gff (https://github.com/Leelouh/pal2gff/blob/main/pal2gff.py), using as parameters a repeat size between 5 and 15 nucleotides, a spacer between pairs of inverted repeats (IRs) of 2 to 10 nucleotides, and a number of mismatches within repeats ranging from 0 to 1. These parameters were chosen taking into account those of the inner IRs found at ends of invertebrate pbles.

data.title: Annotation of inverted repeats displaying features of pble STIR or IR in the hg38, mm10, galGal5 and dr11 genome models

metadata.title: Annotation Coding Environment (ACE) for collaborative qualitative coding
metadata.description: Annotation Coding Environment (ACE) is open-source desktop research software for collaborative qualitative coding of text. It supports transparent, local-first workflows for importing textual sources, developing shared codebooks, applying and reviewing coded annotations, and comparing coding across coders. ACE is designed for research and teaching teams that need a lightweight, inspectable alternative to hosted qualitative analysis platforms. Projects are stored as local .ace files, codebooks can be iteratively refined, and coded material can be exported for reporting, checking, preservation, or downstream analysis in tools such as R, Python, Excel, and other tabular-data environments. Built-in coder agreement summaries help teams examine consistency and discuss interpretive differences while keeping methodological judgement with the researchers. ACE is relevant to qualitative research, mixed-methods studies, content analysis, thematic analysis, and other workflows requiring systematic text annotation, collaborative coding, codebook development, and reproducible research software outputs.
